sentences.text: Well, at that point, since they didn't see the star, they even gave us seats because they were with children.
And a few minutes later, maybe an hour later-- I don't know, because as a child you don't remember time so well-- somebody noticed the star.
And immediately they, they threw us off the seats.
They threw us off the seats.
And they were screaming and yelling at us, dirty Jew and stinking Jew and who needs you?
And you should drop dead and your kids should drop dead.
I mean, terrible, terrible curses and everything.
And the Hungarian soldiers were on the train, too.
And they came.
And instead of helping us out, they were, in unison with the rest of the train, they were going to throw us off the train, the moving train.
And at that point some Serb soldiers showed up.
I Don't know how old these people were on the train, but they were.
And the Serbs were saying to the Hungarians, what kind of people are you?
You want to throw innocent little kids off the train?
You're supposed to keep order here, not to help the crowds riot here.
And so then the Hungarians were, of course, greater in number than the Serbs, and they were telling them, what are you, Jew lovers?
What do you care what we do with these stinking Jews?
And so a fight broke out, and my father said to my mother, let's get as fast out of here as we can, because you know what happens.
At the end, it's always the Jew who's gonna get it.
So we made our way through the train from car to car until the last car.
And my father said to my mother, you know what?
I have a very bad premonition.
I think that when the train slows down before we get to Budapest, we should jump the train, not pull into the station with the train.
Because I heard that they just grab people off the train and they never seen again.
So my mother said, what are you, crazy?
I have a child in my arm.
I'm not going to jump from a moving train.
And he said, don't worry.
I'll jump first, you throw me one child.
Then you throw me the other child, and then you jump.
And that's what happened.
And we made our way home somehow.
I don't know how, but we made our way home from the suburbs into our own home.

And then the next day, we heard that everybody-- every Jew, that is, not everybody-- every Jew who happened to have been at the railroad station or its vicinity that time when that train arrived, they were all taken and never heard from again.
So it's just serendipity.
You can't be too smart.
You just can't.
And again, just to show that it wasn't just the Germans, it was the Hungarians as well.
And in some cases, they were even, they were even more vicious.
Anybody, any Hungarian who coveted anything of yours if you were a Jew could go and denounce you and just take over your apartment, your belongings, your business, whatever.
And maybe not at that point yet where I am now in the story, but later on when all hell broke loose, killing a Jew was nothing.
Less than killing a dog.
There were laws against killing animals, but not killing Jews.
It was almost like a patriotic duty to eliminate one more Jew.
Let's have a Judenrein country-- Judenrein, meaning Jew-free.
And by the way, a little note here of my own.
I, as a little child, I could never understand why we were always called [NON-ENGLISH], which means stinking Jew.
Not until this year did I understand where this come from.
I read a book called And in that book, they tell the entire history-- the author tells the entire history of anti-Semitism, how it developed from the time of Christ on.
And in the Middle Ages, he says, Jews were supposed to be identified with the devil.
And, well, we talk about the devil now, too.
The devil here and the devil take you and what have you.
The devil made me do it.
But in the Middle Ages, the devil was like a real thing in, in, in the lives of people.
And they considered a Jew to be the embodiment of the devil.
Now the devil's supposed to have emitted this sulfuric smell.
And the Jews, because they were supposed to have been the incarnation of the devil, they were also supposed to have been emitting this sulfuric, sulfuric smell.

And that's how, from generation to generation, it came down 'stinking Jew.' And I could never understand why, when my family was cleaner and more hygienic, that most of those people who called us stinking Jew.
Well, that's just an aside, you know, from a child's point of view.
Anyways, when we went back to Budapest-- now, this was end of March or so-- at that point, my family knew that something has to be done.
And they were thinking of going anywhere, but nobody wanted us.
My mother actually had a passport to come to this country.
Well, she applied to come to this country.
She had a passport.
She couldn't get a visa.
My father had a passport.
He couldn't get a visa.
My grandparents, nobody.
Nobody wanted us.
And after a while, Jews had to move into certain sections of the city.
And my parents, I believe, decided that we don't want to be identified with the rest of the population.
We don't want to be taken into houses where only Jews live.
So for a great deal of money, they bought false papers.
But they were really good false papers.
I mean, I don't know what offices did it for them, but it was really official stuff and everybody had false papers.
Under what name?
I was Agnes Kovacs.
K-O-V-A, with an accent, C-S.
It means Smith in Hungarian.
And it's just about as common a name as Smith in English.
So I was Agnes Kovacs.
And I was supposed to have been Protestant.
And my sister, who was 2 and 1/2-- not quite, not quite, she wasn't quite 2 yet-- she was called Susanna Kovacs.
And my father had a friend called Regeur Slani, who was a real righteous Christian.
He was-- and I definitely want to talk about him, because he was unique in that country of evil people.
I guess he went to university with my father, but he was older.
And he was a retired colonel in the Hungarian army.
So he could don his uniform any time and walk freely the streets at any time during the, even during the raids and war and any time.
And so after we had the papers, what happened was that my father was going to be hiding out in Mr.
Slani's apartment.
Mr.
Slani, who lived in [NON-ENGLISH] Street-- this I remember.
I don't remember the street I was at, but I remember Mr.
Slani.
And he was an unmarried man without children.
He had a girlfriend, a countess, the Countess Elvira.
And they took my father in.
And he arranged with one of the lieutenants serving under him when he was still active to taking my mother, supposedly as his mistress.
And she, of course, was called Irene Kovacs.
Everybody was Kovacs.
My father was supposed to have been Andrew Kovacs.
He was Armin, but I guess they thought that was too Jewish, et cetera.
Now, there was always the fear that someone else is going to-- someone's going to recognize us.
So the scheme was that, as I said already, my father would be with his friend, my mother with this lieutenant, and we children would be separated.
We would be placed in a foundling home.

And my grandparents, my grandparents Katz, my cousin Peter, and my Aunt Elsa, who Aunt Elsa was my father's younger sister, and her son Peter, and Aunt Roszi was my father's older brother's wife, and Eva, her child, they all went into a place called [NON-ENGLISH].
That was like a sanatorium or a place where people recuperate.
Not really a nursing home, but a sanatorium on the Buda side of Pest.
And they all had false papers.
And they paid a huge sum of money in order to be in this place.

And when the time came and they took people to the ghetto, my other grandmother, Grandma Gluck, or Gluck, Maria, she was taken to the ghetto, as my mother's mother, with her daughter-in-law and two children-- two grandchildren of hers, two children of the daughter-in-law's, my mother's older brother's wife and two children.
Now, when my mother found out that her mother had been taken to the ghetto, she went ballistic.
She was hysterical.

She went to my other grandmother, who was safely, supposedly, in the sanatorium, and said to her, if you are not going to buy my mother out of the ghetto and put her here with you with the rest of the family, I'm going to denounce you.
So my other grandmother put up the money.
And this grandmother of mine also went there.
And just to show you how things didn't work the way people think they work, at the end of the story you'll see what a mistake my mother made.
And anyway, so now the one grandfather was away already in slave labor.
My uncles were all in slave labor camps, some of them in as far as Russia, some of them just in the vicinity of Budapest.
And now my mother was living with this army officer and my father separately.
And Uncle Regeur we called him, Mr.
Slani, every day went to my mother and carried kosher food to my father.
Because my father, although he was modern, he was so religious he didn't want to eat non-kosher.
Now, you know, it wasn't a matter of kosher meat, because there was no kosher meat.
There was no meat, period.
But my mother cooked with kosher dishes and kosher stuff.
So that's all my father ate.
And this wonderful man risked his life every day bringing food to my father.
Plus, of course, he was the one who went for food shopping and stuff.
And around June-- I'm regressing a little, because around June, we were still living our own place.
I, I never was taken to any ghetto or any place.
Before we were supposed to move out, they already gave us away.
So, in other words, my parents placed us.
So in June, early June, a woman came to get my sister first and took her away.
And a couple of weeks later, she came for me.
At this point already, my parents knew that the countryside was Judenrein.
Almost all Jews were deported by then, even some of the Jews from the outskirts of Budapest.
Many of my relatives remained alive who were residents of Budapest, and almost none who were in the suburbs.
Like, you know, you are in the suburb of New York.
That kind of suburb I'm talking about.
So my parents felt they had no choice.
And in order so that no one recognizes us, we should be as far away from them as possible.
So this lady, called Mrs.
[NON-ENGLSIH], I thought at the time she was an old woman, but probably she was half my age.
I don't know.
She came and she was going to take me with her to her house where my sister was.
And I remember that my mother washed my hair with something like gasoline or petroleum gel.
I don't know.
No, petroleum gel is Vaseline.
Something gasoline-smelling, because the peasant girls very often had lice, and for lack of any other lice killer, they washed their hair in this.
So in order for me to smell like a peasant girl, she washed my hair in that.
And I had long curls that were always made the Shirley Temple curls.
And she braided my hair, put it on top of my head.
And I remember I put on a peasant dress and a peasant's kerchief-- polka-dotted peasant kerchief.
And I was told that Mommy and Daddy love you very much, but you have to go away.
And you know that you're Jewish, but you can never, ever tell anybody that you're Jewish because you'll get killed.

And I was, of course, drilled already for weeks as to what my name was, where my father was, who my mother was, and all the prayers, the Protestant prayers, and the songs, and Christmas songs, and everything.
I knew it by heart like you wouldn't believe it.
And I was also told not to cry, not to make a big scene, because we don't want the neighbors to know that you're being taken away permanently.
Well, they didn't say permanently, but for a long period of time.
And Mommy and Daddy will come and get you when everything is OK.
Now, I wasn't yet four years old.
So I, I really didn't know what it meant to be dead or killed, but I knew it was something horrible.
And I remember this woman picked me up.
And I had a small suitcase.
And she took the suitcase in one hand, me in the other hand.
And she told my mother, don't come with us to the tram stop.
And she was heading towards the tram.
And I was looking back over her shoulder and I saw my mother.
And my mother was, like, hiding From-- she was following us.
And from house to house, she would duck into the entrances of the houses so she thought I didn't see her, but I did.
And I could see that she was crying.
And then we got on the tram, and I saw her from the tram.
And I was holding on to this woman like for dear life, because I didn't know.
Where am I going?
What am I doing here?
I mean, I was told, but as a kid, it doesn't quite penetrate why.
Why?
Why do I have to do this?
And I saw my mother, and as the tram was going away, I saw less and less of her.
And it just-- even when I think of it now, it was a horrible, horrible feeling.
I thought maybe I'll never see her again, which could have been the case.
My father wasn't present.
I guess maybe he couldn't take the separation.
I don't know.
I don't know.
So we took the tram.
And this lady lived in Pestszentlorinc.
Don't ask me to spell it, because it's useless.
No one is going to be able to spell it.
It's one word, believe it or not.
And she was a very poor woman.
She kept orphans and people who were abandoned.
I guess that's what a foundling home is.
And of course she was paid for us.
And I don't know if she was paid for the other children or not.

And when I arrived there, from the kind of luxurious lifestyle that I was used to this one-floor hovel that she lived in and all these dirty kids, and I walk into this dingy place, and the far end of the room, there's my sister sitting on the potty.
And all of the sudden-- she couldn't speak yet when she left home.
All of a sudden, her eyes lit up, and she's smiling, and she's calling my name, and she's extending her arms, Agica, Agica.
And I ran to her.
And from then on, I had this feeling that I'm the older one, I'm responsible.
And the two of us went through everything together, my sister and I.
So things were going on at this place.
It was summer.
It wasn't cold yet.
It was, it was all right.
The woman was very poor and there wasn't very much food.
Uncle Regeur came every week to bring food.
But my parents sent such luxurious items, like oranges and chocolate and stuff like that.
So I never got any of it, because the woman had to support all this other seven or eight kids.
So she sold it on the black market and she bought black bread or whatever.
So I wasn't starving, but I wasn't very well-fed either.
But she did her best.
She was a good woman.
She was very good woman, and I wish I could ever thank her.
She is probably dead by now.
And then the bombings got more frequent.
This is 1944, July, August.
And I don't remember who the other children were.
I only remember one mentally retarded girl whose name was Pessi.
And she was about 16 or so and didn't have more brains than I did at four, but nice kid.
And what I remember is we had these air raids.
And I was always petrified.
I just, I couldn't help it.
I, I was so afraid of bombs.
I was afraid of dying.
And what they had-- what Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH] had was an underground bunker.
It was literally just earth dug out.
And when the airplanes came, we would crawl in there.
An adult couldn't stand up in there.
And it was damp, and it was-- there was no light in there, of course.
It was underground.
And we just sat there and waited out the air raid.
One, on time we weren't so lucky.
The house was demolished.
And I don't know how this woman got word to my parents that there's no more house above our head, but Uncle Regeur came and he was going to take us back.
And this woman knew Uncle Regeur.
And yet, she said to him, I am personally responsible for these children.
I have to hand them over to the parents.
I can't give them to anybody.
And she knew this man.
So I remember that she was hurt in the raid.
She-- a shell hit her, I think the left foot.
She could barely walk, but she came with us.
She couldn't wear shoes.
She bundled up her feet in rags and she came with us.
And we had to walk for a while.
I think that Uncle Regeur came in an army uniform in order to pass all checkpoints and everything.
And he came with an army ambulance.
But in order to get to the army ambulance, I don't know why, we had to walk through the main street of the town.
And when we arrived there, they shunted us to the sidewalk.
And all of a sudden, in the middle of the street, it was, it was a dusty, real small-town main street where the dust was as thick as fog.
And this is the end of the summer.
And I see this ragged bunch of people, hundreds of people, all ages.
There were men and women and children, and some of them carrying bundles, and some of them carrying a little suitcase, and some of them pushing a baby cart, I mean, baby carriage.
And they were the Nazis herding them with whips and with the butts of the rifles.
And I was standing on the corner of the street.
And I hear cheering from all over.
The streets were lined with the non-Jews who are watching this.
Nobody came to the aid of these people.
Instead, what they did, they were yelling at them, stinking Jews, drop dead.
Who needs you?
You are rotten Jews.
You get what you deserve.
I mean, I realized-- at that point, I realized these were Jews.
These were my people.
And then I saw a woman holding a little girl by the hand about my age.
Maybe a little older, I don't know.
And it just hit me, it could be me.
If I tell anyone that I am a Jew, I could be there instead of that little girl.
And I was just-- I can't explain the feeling to you.
This was my first real encounter with the Nazis, what they did to the Jews.
And seeing the faces of these people-- I mean, as a four-year-old you really don't understand a hell of a lot.
But you know that nobody was laughing.
And you saw these people looking at the ground and people spitting at them.
And it was just heartbreaking.
